title: Neck of Femur Fracture
oneLiner: A fragility fracture of the proximal femur in osteoporotic older adults, requiring urgent surgical repair to reduce morbidity and mortality.

sections.Killers First — What Will Kill Them In T:
- **Fat embolism syndrome** – sudden onset hypoxia, petechiae, confusion within minutes of injury (especially with intramedullary nailing).
  - *Finding:* Acute respiratory distress + petechiae on chest/axillae + unexplained confusion.
- **Acute coronary syndrome / high-output cardiac failure** – stress of injury + anaemia + pain can unmask critical coronary stenosis in frail elderly.
  - *Finding:* Chest pain or shortness of breath + new ECG changes (ST depression, T inversion) + raised troponin.
- **Major haemorrhage** – especially if on anticoagulation (warfarin, DOACs) or with subtrochanteric extension.
  - *Finding:* Tachycardia + hypotension + falling Hb/haematocrit despite fluid resuscitation; check INR/anti-Xa urgently.
sections.Presentation:
- Inability to bear weight after a fall (often low-energy, from standing height)
- Pain localised to hip/groin (may radiate to knee)
- Shortened and externally rotated leg (classic sign for displaced fracture)
- Tenderness over hip joint on palpation or gentle logging
- Possible confusion (delirium may be the only presentation in dementia patients)
sections.Differential Diagnosis:
- **Pelvic fracture** – pain on lateral compression; pelvic X-ray shows disruption; no shortening/rotation
- **Trochanteric bursitis / greater trochanteric pain syndrome** – can bear weight, no shortening; pain on palpation of lateral hip
- **Osteoarthritis flare** – history of OA, gradual onset, no trauma; leg not short/rotated
- **Referred low back pain (lumbar radiculopathy)** – back signs, straight leg raise positive, hip exam normal
- **Splitter:** "Shortened + externally rotated leg = NOF fracture until proven otherwise; any other leg position should raise suspicion for alternate diagnosis."
sections.Investigations:
- **Bedside:**
  - ECG (pre-op baseline; detect ACS, AF)
  - Oxygen saturations – baseline; if <94%, think fat embolism or pneumonia
  - Bladder scan (rule out retention which mimics confusion)
- **Bloods:**
  - FBC, U&E, LFTs, clotting screen (INR, APTT)
  - Group & Save (or cross-match if Hb <100 g/L or active bleeding)
  - CRP, bone profile (calcium, albumin)
  - Troponin (if cardiac concern or pre-op risk)
  - COVID-19 swab (per local policy)
- **Imaging:**
  - **First-line:** Pelvis AP + cross-table lateral of affected hip (or dedicated hip series)
  - **Second-line:** CT hip if X-ray negative but high clinical suspicion (e.g., impacted fracture) – modern CT has ~100% sensitivity
  - **MRI:** For occult fractures (especially in young/traumatic cases) – better for early diagnosis
  - **None:** Routine chest X-ray only if cardiopulmonary symptoms
- **Diagnostic criteria / scoring system:**
  - Garden classification (I–IV) for intracapsular; Jensen classification for trochanteric
  - **Never-miss test:** **CT hip** if X-ray negative and patient cannot weight bear – missing an occult fracture leads to displacement, avascular necrosis, and litigation.
sections.Management:
- **Immediate (first hour / ED):**
  - Pain control: paracetamol 1 g IV/PO (if not contraindicated) + morphine 2.5–5 mg IV PRN (watch for hypotension, especially in elderly)
  - IV access + fluids: 500–1000 mL crystalloid (e.g., Hartmann’s) if hypotensive
  - Check and reverse anticoagulation (vitamin K 1–5 mg IV for warfarin; idarucizumab for dabigatran, [check local policy] for apixaban/rivarelbant/edoxaban)
  - NBM (surgery expected within 36h – NICE)
  - Urinary catheter only if strict Output monitoring needed (otherwise avoid – increases infection risk)
- **Ward (first 24–72h):**
  - **Surgery:** Within 36h of admission (NICE) – cannulated screws or hemiarthroplasty (intracapsular), DHS or intramedullary nail (extracapsular)
  - Orthogeriatric co-care (daily review)
  - Mobilise day 1 post-op (unless hip precautions for hemiarthroplasty)
  - **VTE prophylaxis:** LMWH ([check local policy] for dose, e.g., enoxaparin 40mg SC OD) – start 6–12h post-op; continue for 28 days
  - **Delirium:** haloperidol 0.5 mg PO/IM PRN (avoid in Parkinson’s/Lewy body dementia)
- **Long-term / outpatient:**
  - Falls assessment + bone health (bisphosphonate, calcium/vitamin D)
  - Home physio / cardiac rehab
- **Default Orders (if admitted):**
  - Fluids: Maintenance crystalloid (e.g., 0.9% saline + 5% dextrose at 84 mL/h) until eating for 48h
  - VTE prophylaxis: Enoxaparin 40 mg SC OD (renal adjustment: CrCl <30 → 20 mg OD)
  - Diet: NBM pending surgery; after surgery – free fluids advancing to soft diet PRN
  - Observations: Baseline 4-hourly (NEWS2); more frequent if NEWS2 ≥5
sections.Drugs & Doses:
- **First-line agents:**
  - Paracetamol 1 g QDS PO/IV – safe first-line
  - Morphine 2.5–10 mg IM/SC/IV PRN (max 30 mg/4h) – caution in elderly with CKD
  - LMWH: Enoxaparin 40 mg SC OD (prophylaxis) – [check local policy] as some use dalteparin/tinzaparin
- **Common alternatives:**
  - Oxycodone IR 2.5–5 mg PO PRN (for intolerable pain)
  - NSAIDs (e.g., ibuprofen 400 mg TDS) – avoid in elderly with AKI/peptic ulcer/antiplatelet therapy
- **Key contraindications / interactions:**
  - Warfarin + NSAIDs → bleeding risk
  - Anticholinergics (e.g., procyclidine) in delirium – avoid; use olanzapine instead
- **Renal/hepatic adjustment:**
  - Enoxaparin: CrCl <30 → 20 mg SC OD
  - Morphine: reduce dose and avoid sustained-release if eGFR <30
  - Paracetamol: safe, but caution in severe liver disease
- **Most dangerous dose error:**
  - **LMWH overdose in elderly with AKI** → major bleeding → always document eGFR and adjust dose; use weight-based dosing for therapeutic anticoagulation.
sections.Complications:
- **Acute:**
  - Venous thromboembolism (DVT/PE) – standard prophylaxis
  - Pneumonia (immobility + aspiration post-op)
  - Acute kidney injury (dehydration + contrast + NSAIDs)
  - Fat embolism syndrome (especially with intramedullary nailing)
  - Haemorrhage (haematoma, wound bleeding)
  - Urinary retention / catheter-associated UTI
  - Delirium (up to 50%)
- **Chronic:**
  - Avascular necrosis (intracapsular fractures – risk depends on displacement and time to fixation)
  - Non-union (more common in subcapital fractures)
  - Implant failure / cut-out (trochanteric fractures)
  - Loss of independent mobility (1-year mortality 20–30%; many never walk unaided again)
  - Chronic pain, pressure sores, contractures
sections.Prognosis: - 1-year mortality 20–30% (higher if >85, male, multimorbid, or delayed surgery). Half of survivors lose pre-injury walking ability. The risk of second hip fracture is ~10%.
sections.Pearls (positive actions):
- **Always perform a CT hip if X-ray negative and patient can’t weight bear** – occult NOF fractures are common and delay increases avascular necrosis risk.
- **Start LMWH **before** surgery (12h pre-op) unless spinal anaesthesia is planned** – reduces VTE risk without increasing bleeding if given properly.
- **Order a dedicated DXA scan after discharge** – but don’t delay surgery for it; start calcium/vitamin D while awaiting results.
sections.Pitfalls (Errors To Avoid):
- **Do not delay surgery for pre-op cardiac “clearance”** – the stress of delay outweighs risk; senior anaesthetist + orthogeriatrician make the decision.
- **Avoid urinary catheter unless essential** – it doubles UTI risk; use bladder scanner to check retention before straight catheter.
- **Do not give NSAIDs in the elderly** – AKI and GI bleeding risk far outweighs benefit; use paracetamol + opioids.
sections.Safety Netting (If Patient Goes Home):
- **Return if:** New shortness of breath, calf pain/swelling (DVT/PE), fever/shivering (wound infection), severe hip pain unrelieved by analgesia, inability to weight bear after surgery, confusion worse than baseline.
- **Follow-up:** Fracture clinic at 6–8 weeks for X-ray + clinical review; orthogeriatric clinic at 12 weeks for falls prevention and bone health.
sections.Escalation Criteria:
- **NEWS2 ≥5** → review and escalate to ward registrar/medical team within 30 minutes
- **NEWS2 ≥7** → immediate clinical review and consider HDU/ITU
- **Lactate >4 mmol/L** (after adequate fluids) → consider sepsis/VTE → call medical registrar
- **GCS drop >2 points** → urgent CT head (subdural haemorrhage from fall) and blood glucose check
- **Any new ECG change with chest pain** → stat troponin + ECG → cardiology referral; avoid beta-blockers in acute phase

rawMarkdown:
## Neck of Femur Fracture
**One-liner:** A fragility fracture of the proximal femur in osteoporotic older adults, requiring urgent surgical repair to reduce morbidity and mortality.

### Killers First — What Will Kill Them In The First Hour?
- **Fat embolism syndrome** – sudden onset hypoxia, petechiae, confusion within minutes of injury (especially with intramedullary nailing).
  - *Finding:* Acute respiratory distress + petechiae on chest/axillae + unexplained confusion.
- **Acute coronary syndrome / high-output cardiac failure** – stress of injury + anaemia + pain can unmask critical coronary stenosis in frail elderly.
  - *Finding:* Chest pain or shortness of breath + new ECG changes (ST depression, T inversion) + raised troponin.
- **Major haemorrhage** – especially if on anticoagulation (warfarin, DOACs) or with subtrochanteric extension.
  - *Finding:* Tachycardia + hypotension + falling Hb/haematocrit despite fluid resuscitation; check INR/anti-Xa urgently.

### Presentation
- Inability to bear weight after a fall (often low-energy, from standing height)
- Pain localised to hip/groin (may radiate to knee)
- Shortened and externally rotated leg (classic sign for displaced fracture)
- Tenderness over hip joint on palpation or gentle logging
- Possible confusion (delirium may be the only presentation in dementia patients)

### Differential Diagnosis
- **Pelvic fracture** – pain on lateral compression; pelvic X-ray shows disruption; no shortening/rotation
- **Trochanteric bursitis / greater trochanteric pain syndrome** – can bear weight, no shortening; pain on palpation of lateral hip
- **Osteoarthritis flare** – history of OA, gradual onset, no trauma; leg not short/rotated
- **Referred low back pain (lumbar radiculopathy)** – back signs, straight leg raise positive, hip exam normal
- **Splitter:** "Shortened + externally rotated leg = NOF fracture until proven otherwise; any other leg position should raise suspicion for alternate diagnosis."

### Investigations
- **Bedside:**
  - ECG (pre-op baseline; detect ACS, AF)
  - Oxygen saturations – baseline; if <94%, think fat embolism or pneumonia
  - Bladder scan (rule out retention which mimics confusion)
- **Bloods:**
  - FBC, U&E, LFTs, clotting screen (INR, APTT)
  - Group & Save (or cross-match if Hb <100 g/L or active bleeding)
  - CRP, bone profile (calcium, albumin)
  - Troponin (if cardiac concern or pre-op risk)
  - COVID-19 swab (per local policy)
- **Imaging:**
  - **First-line:** Pelvis AP + cross-table lateral of affected hip (or dedicated hip series)
  - **Second-line:** CT hip if X-ray negative but high clinical suspicion (e.g., impacted fracture) – modern CT has ~100% sensitivity
  - **MRI:** For occult fractures (especially in young/traumatic cases) – better for early diagnosis
  - **None:** Routine chest X-ray only if cardiopulmonary symptoms
- **Diagnostic criteria / scoring system:**
  - Garden classification (I–IV) for intracapsular; Jensen classification for trochanteric
  - **Never-miss test:** **CT hip** if X-ray negative and patient cannot weight bear – missing an occult fracture leads to displacement, avascular necrosis, and litigation.

### Management
- **Immediate (first hour / ED):**
  - Pain control: paracetamol 1 g IV/PO (if not contraindicated) + morphine 2.5–5 mg IV PRN (watch for hypotension, especially in elderly)
  - IV access + fluids: 500–1000 mL crystalloid (e.g., Hartmann’s) if hypotensive
  - Check and reverse anticoagulation (vitamin K 1–5 mg IV for warfarin; idarucizumab for dabigatran, [check local policy] for apixaban/rivarelbant/edoxaban)
  - NBM (surgery expected within 36h – NICE)
  - Urinary catheter only if strict Output monitoring needed (otherwise avoid – increases infection risk)
- **Ward (first 24–72h):**
  - **Surgery:** Within 36h of admission (NICE) – cannulated screws or hemiarthroplasty (intracapsular), DHS or intramedullary nail (extracapsular)
  - Orthogeriatric co-care (daily review)
  - Mobilise day 1 post-op (unless hip precautions for hemiarthroplasty)
  - **VTE prophylaxis:** LMWH ([check local policy] for dose, e.g., enoxaparin 40mg SC OD) – start 6–12h post-op; continue for 28 days
  - **Delirium:** haloperidol 0.5 mg PO/IM PRN (avoid in Parkinson’s/Lewy body dementia)
- **Long-term / outpatient:**
  - Falls assessment + bone health (bisphosphonate, calcium/vitamin D)
  - Home physio / cardiac rehab
- **Default Orders (if admitted):**
  - Fluids: Maintenance crystalloid (e.g., 0.9% saline + 5% dextrose at 84 mL/h) until eating for 48h
  - VTE prophylaxis: Enoxaparin 40 mg SC OD (renal adjustment: CrCl <30 → 20 mg OD)
  - Diet: NBM pending surgery; after surgery – free fluids advancing to soft diet PRN
  - Observations: Baseline 4-hourly (NEWS2); more frequent if NEWS2 ≥5

### Drugs & Doses
- **First-line agents:**
  - Paracetamol 1 g QDS PO/IV – safe first-line
  - Morphine 2.5–10 mg IM/SC/IV PRN (max 30 mg/4h) – caution in elderly with CKD
  - LMWH: Enoxaparin 40 mg SC OD (prophylaxis) – [check local policy] as some use dalteparin/tinzaparin
- **Common alternatives:**
  - Oxycodone IR 2.5–5 mg PO PRN (for intolerable pain)
  - NSAIDs (e.g., ibuprofen 400 mg TDS) – avoid in elderly with AKI/peptic ulcer/antiplatelet therapy
- **Key contraindications / interactions:**
  - Warfarin + NSAIDs → bleeding risk
  - Anticholinergics (e.g., procyclidine) in delirium – avoid; use olanzapine instead
- **Renal/hepatic adjustment:**
  - Enoxaparin: CrCl <30 → 20 mg SC OD
  - Morphine: reduce dose and avoid sustained-release if eGFR <30
  - Paracetamol: safe, but caution in severe liver disease
- **Most dangerous dose error:**
  - **LMWH overdose in elderly with AKI** → major bleeding → always document eGFR and adjust dose; use weight-based dosing for therapeutic anticoagulation.

### Complications
- **Acute:**
  - Venous thromboembolism (DVT/PE) – standard prophylaxis
  - Pneumonia (immobility + aspiration post-op)
  - Acute kidney injury (dehydration + contrast + NSAIDs)
  - Fat embolism syndrome (especially with intramedullary nailing)
  - Haemorrhage (haematoma, wound bleeding)
  - Urinary retention / catheter-associated UTI
  - Delirium (up to 50%)
- **Chronic:**
  - Avascular necrosis (intracapsular fractures – risk depends on displacement and time to fixation)
  - Non-union (more common in subcapital fractures)
  - Implant failure / cut-out (trochanteric fractures)
  - Loss of independent mobility (1-year mortality 20–30%; many never walk unaided again)
  - Chronic pain, pressure sores, contractures

### Prognosis
- 1-year mortality 20–30% (higher if >85, male, multimorbid, or delayed surgery). Half of survivors lose pre-injury walking ability. The risk of second hip fracture is ~10%.

### Pearls (positive actions)
- **Always perform a CT hip if X-ray negative and patient can’t weight bear** – occult NOF fractures are common and delay increases avascular necrosis risk.
- **Start LMWH **before** surgery (12h pre-op) unless spinal anaesthesia is planned** – reduces VTE risk without increasing bleeding if given properly.
- **Order a dedicated DXA scan after discharge** – but don’t delay surgery for it; start calcium/vitamin D while awaiting results.

### Pitfalls (Errors To Avoid)
- **Do not delay surgery for pre-op cardiac “clearance”** – the stress of delay outweighs risk; senior anaesthetist + orthogeriatrician make the decision.
- **Avoid urinary catheter unless essential** – it doubles UTI risk; use bladder scanner to check retention before straight catheter.
- **Do not give NSAIDs in the elderly** – AKI and GI bleeding risk far outweighs benefit; use paracetamol + opioids.

### Safety Netting (If Patient Goes Home)
- **Return if:** New shortness of breath, calf pain/swelling (DVT/PE), fever/shivering (wound infection), severe hip pain unrelieved by analgesia, inability to weight bear after surgery, confusion worse than baseline.
- **Follow-up:** Fracture clinic at 6–8 weeks for X-ray + clinical review; orthogeriatric clinic at 12 weeks for falls prevention and bone health.

### Escalation Criteria
- **NEWS2 ≥5** → review and escalate to ward registrar/medical team within 30 minutes
- **NEWS2 ≥7** → immediate clinical review and consider HDU/ITU
- **Lactate >4 mmol/L** (after adequate fluids) → consider sepsis/VTE → call medical registrar
- **GCS drop >2 points** → urgent CT head (subdural haemorrhage from fall) and blood glucose check
- **Any new ECG change with chest pain** → stat troponin + ECG → cardiology referral; avoid beta-blockers in acute phase
